hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Speaking Up with Confidence -- Hotels, Reservations, and When Things Go Wrong
lesson.menu_desc: Do check-in ao problema na conta: pedidos educados e perguntas indiretas -- como falar firme sem soar grosseira -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 7 -- Speaking Up with Confidence
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 7 -- Speaking Up with Confidence

listenings.text: Good afternoon, Ms. Ito, this is Diane from the front desk at the Bryant Park Hotel, returning your call. I have some news about your stay, and I want to sort a few things out before you arrive. First, the good news. We were not able to hold the standard room you booked, so I have moved you to a junior suite on the fourteenth floor, at no extra charge. It is a bit larger, and it overlooks the park. I hope that works for you. Now, about the two questions you left on your message. You asked whether the room would be ready before three. It will. Housekeeping finishes the upper floors by one, so if you arrive early, just come to the desk and we will check you in. You also asked about the parking surcharge on your confirmation. That is a valet fee of forty-two dollars a night, and I completely understand why you would want to remove it, because you are not bringing a car. I have already taken it off. One last thing. I saw the note that you would like a late checkout on your last day. I cannot promise it yet, but if you remind us the night before, we will do everything we can. Please call back if there is anything else. We look forward to having you with us.

Welcome back to The Careful Traveler. Today, one small idea that has saved me more money and more bad nights than anything else: how you complain at a hotel decides what you get. Here is the thing nobody tells you. The person at the front desk can do almost anything for you, and they will do it for the guest who is calm, and they will not do it for the guest who is loud. Same problem, same desk, two completely different results. So here is my rule. Never start with the problem. Start with a question. Instead of, this room is filthy, try, would you mind sending someone up to take another look at the room? Instead of, I want a refund, try, I was wondering if you could help me sort out the charge on my bill. You are saying the exact same thing, but now you are on the same side of the desk. And one more, because this is the one people get wrong. When you ask an indirect question, keep the words in the normal order. It is, do you know if the pool is open, not, do you know is the pool open. It sounds small. It is not. The soft version is the one that gets the upgrade, gets the late checkout, gets the surcharge removed. Be kind, be specific, and ask like you expect a yes.

items.situation: You arrive at 1 p.m. and you want to know if you can check in early. Ask the front desk -- politely.
tips: Do you know if the room is ready yet? I arrived a little early.
Indirect question -- normal word order: 'if the room is ready', never 'is the room ready'.

items.situation: The heating in your room does not work. It is your first night. Ask them to send someone -- do not order.
tips: Would you mind sending someone up to look at the heating?
After 'would you mind', the verb takes -ing: sending, not to send.

items.situation: There is a forty-two dollar valet surcharge on your bill, but you did not bring a car. Speak up.
tips: I was wondering if you could take a look at this -- there seems to be a valet charge, and I did not use the parking.
Open with the soft frame, then state the fact. Firm, not aggressive.

items.situation: You want a late checkout on your last day, because your flight leaves at night. Ask.
tips: Could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday? My flight is not until nine in the evening.
'Could I possibly...' is the polite way to ask for a favor for yourself.

items.situation: The room next to yours is very loud at midnight. You call the front desk. Ask them to help -- calmly.
tips: I'm sorry to call so late, but would you mind asking the guests next door to keep it down?
A short apology plus a soft request opens the door. Nobody says no to that.

items.situation: The receptionist has just explained the breakfast times and the checkout policy, fast. Close it the safe way.
tips: Let me make sure I got that: breakfast until ten, checkout at eleven. Is that right?
Confirm before you walk away -- the 'say it back' move from Lesson 3 still works.
